elements.sent-2411-001: We study the Ollivier-Ricci curvature and its modification introduced by Lin, Lu, and Yau on graphs.
elements.sent-2411-002: We provide a complete characterization of all graphs with Lin-Lu-Yau curvature at least one.
elements.sent-2411-003: We then explore the relationship between the Lin-Lu-Yau curvature and the Ollivier-Ricci curvature with vanishing idleness on regular graphs.
elements.sent-2411-004: An exact formula for the difference between these two curvature notions is established, along with an equality condition.
elements.sent-2411-005: This condition allows us to characterize edges that are bone-idle in regular graphs.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we demonstrate the non-existence of 3-regular bone-idle graphs and present a complete characterization of all 4-regular bone-idle graphs.
elements.sent-2411-007: We also show that there exist no 5-regular bone-idle graphs that are symmetric or a Cartesian product of a 3-regular and a 2-regular graph.
elements.sent-2411-008: Since the introduction of the geometric notion of curvature by Gauss and Riemann over 150 years ago, it has played a central role in differential geometry.
elements.sent-2411-009: Among the different types of curvature, Ricci curvature is of particular importance, serving as a fundamental tool in the study of Riemannian manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-010: Given its importance in differential geometry, it is natural to seek extensions of Ricci curvature to broader classes of metric spaces beyond Riemannian manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-011: This pursuit has led to the development of various generalized curvature notions for non-smooth or discrete structures, see, e.g., Bakry-{\'E mery \cite{BE1985 , Erbar-Maas \cite{EM2012 , Mielke \cite{M2013 and Forman \cite{F2003 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this work, we study a notion of Ricci-curvature introduced by Ollivier in 2009 \cite{Ollivier2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Von Renesse and Sturm \cite{RS2005 established a connection between Ricci curvature and optimal transport on smooth Riemannian manifolds.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Building upon their results, Ollivier developed a discrete notion of Ricci curvature on metric spaces equipped with Markov chains or a measure, known as the Ollivier-Ricci curvature.
elements.sent-2411-015: This approach leverages optimal transport theory, as Ollivier's definition of curvature is based on the Wasserstein distance.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the context of locally finite graphs, Ollivier's notion of Ricci curvature has recently received considerable attention.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this setting, the Ollivier-Ricci curvature $\kappa_{\alpha}$ is defined on the edges of the graph and depends on an idleness parameter $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-019: In 2011, Lin, Lu, and Yau \cite{LLY2011 introduced a modification of the Ollivier-Ricci curvature, by computing the derivative of the curvature with respect to the idleness parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-020: We will refer to this modification as Lin-Lu-Yau curvature and denote it by $\kappa$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Our first result is a complete characterization of the graphs with Lin-Lu-Yau curvature greater than or equal to one for every edge.
elements.sent-2411-022: Then $\kappa(x,y) \geq 1$ for every edge $x\sim y\in E$ if and only if the minimum degree $\delta(G) \geq \vert V \vert -2$.
elements.sent-2411-023: We then focus on the relationship between the $0$-Ollivier-Ricci curvature $\kappa_{0}$ and the Lin-Lu-Yau curvature $\kappa$.
elements.sent-2411-024: For regular graphs, we derive an exact formula for the difference between these two curvature notions.

elements.sent-2411-028: Finally, we study an analog of Ricci-flat manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-029: Ricci-flat Lorentzian manifolds play an important role in theoretical physics as solutions to Einstein's field equations in a vacuum with vanishing cosmological constant.
elements.sent-2411-030: As an analog, one might consider graphs where the Ollivier-Ricci curvature vanishes everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this work, we impose an even stronger condition.
elements.sent-2411-032: Namely, that the Ollivier-Ricci curvature $\kappa_{\alpha}$ vanishes everywhere for every idleness parameter $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-033: We refer to such graphs as bone-idle.
elements.sent-2411-034: It turns out that bone-idleness is equivalent to the vanishing of both the $0$-Ollivier-Ricci curvature and the Lin-Lu-Yau curvature $\kappa $ everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-035: Therefore, we can apply our results on the relation between the Ollivier-Ricci curvature and the Lin-Lu-Yau curvature.
elements.sent-2411-036: Using this, we characterize edges that are bone-idle in regular graphs.
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore, we show that no 3-regular bone-idle graph exists.
elements.sent-2411-038: Suppose that $G$ is bone-idle, then $G$ is not $3$-regular.
elements.sent-2411-039: We also provide a complete characterization of 4-regular bone-idle graphs in Section \ref{bone_idle_4_reg and discuss the existence of 5-regular bone-idle graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-040: We conclude this introduction by providing an outline of the remainder of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-041: In Section \ref{defs_and_notations we review the relevant concepts of Graph Theory and Optimal Transport Theory, and introduce the Ollivier-Ricci curvature, as well as its modification by Lin, Lu, and Yau.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In Section \ref{lly_geq_1 we provide a complete characterization of all graphs with Lin-Lu-Yau curvature at least one.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section \ref{relation_curvatures we explore the relationship between the Lin-Lu-Yau curvature and the $0$-Ollivier-Ricci curvature on regular graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Finally, in Section \ref{bone_idleness , we present our findings on bone-idle graphs.}

elements.sent-2411-047: A simple graph $G=(V,E)$ is an unweighted, undirected graph that contains no multiple edges or self-loops.

elements.sent-2411-050: If no such path exists, $d(x,y)$ is defined to be infinity.
elements.sent-2411-051: The diameter of $G$ is denoted by $diam(G) = \max_{x,y \in V d(x,y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The girth of $G$ is the length of a shortest cycle contained in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-053: If $G$ does not contain any cycles, the girth is defined to be infinity.

elements.sent-2411-060: An automorphism of a graph $G=(V,E)$ is a permutation $\sigma$ of the vertex set $V$ such that for any pair of vertices $x,y \in V$, $x\sim y$ if and only if $\sigma(x) \sim \sigma(y)$.
elements.sent-2411-061: In other words, an automorphism is an isomorphism of $G$ to itself.
elements.sent-2411-062: An edge automorphism of $G=(V,E)$ is a permutation of the edge set $E$ that sends edges with a common endpoint into edges with a common endpoint.
elements.sent-2411-063: A graph is called vertex-transitive if, for any two vertices $x,y\in V$, there exists an automorphism $\sigma$ of the graph such that $\sigma(x) = y$.

elements.sent-2411-069: The Wasserstein distance, a metric defined on the space of probability measures, is a fundamental concept in optimal transport theory.

elements.sent-2411-071: The Wasserstein distance between $\mu_{1}$ and $\mu_{2}$ is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:1} W_{1}(\mu_{1}, \mu_{2}) = \inf_{\pi \in \Pi(\mu_{1}, \mu_{2})} \sum_{x \in V} \sum_{y \in V} d(x,y) \pi(x,y),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Pi(\mu_{1}, \mu_{2}) = \left \{ \pi: V \times V \to [0,1]: \sum_{y \in V} \pi(x,y)=\mu_{1}(x), \; \sum_{x \in V} \pi(x,y)=\mu_{2}(y) \right \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-072: Intuitively, imagine two distributions given by $\mu_{1}$ and $\mu_{2}$ as piles of earth.
